outcomes.consensus_answer: Maybe, but the effect is unclear. The cross-sectional study reported no cases of AIS in the HPV vaccine group; one pre-post vaccine introduction study reported an increase in AIS incidence, while the other reported a reduction.

outcomes.question: Does full or partial series HPV vaccination in the general population reduce adenocarcinoma in situ (AIS) incidence relative to no vaccination?

outcomes.consensus_answer: Maybe, but the effect is unclear. The RCT extension study reported no cases of vulval cancer in vaccinated participants; two pre-post vaccine introduction studies reported decreased vulval cancer incidence, one reported increased incidence, and one reported inconsistent results across ethnic groups.

outcomes.question: Does full or partial series HPV vaccination in the general population reduce invasive vulval cancer incidence relative to no vaccination?

outcomes.consensus_answer: No. The evidence suggests that HPV vaccination does not increase the risk of Guillain-Barré syndrome. Nine cohort studies reported no association between HPV vaccination and increased risk of Guillain-Barré syndrome (RR 0.89, 95% CI 0.36 to 2.20), although one cohort study reported an increase in incidence associated with HPV vaccination. The self-controlled case series analyses reported no increased risk following HPV vaccination (RR 1.53, 95% CI 0.78 to 2.98).

outcomes.question: In children with presumed pulmonary tuberculosis, does Xpert Ultra performed on sputum detect pulmonary tuberculosis relative to culture as the reference standard?

outcomes.consensus_answer: Yes, the summary sensitivity is 69.6% (95% CI: 60.3% to 77.6%).

outcomes.consensus_answer: Yes, the summary specificity is 91.0% (95% CI: 82.5% to 95.6%).
outcomes.question: In children with presumed pulmonary tuberculosis, does Xpert Ultra performed on gastric aspirate correctly identify children without pulmonary tuberculosis relative to culture as the reference standard?

outcomes.consensus_answer: Yes, the summary sensitivity is 68.0% (95% CI: 50.3% to 81.7%).

outcomes.consensus_answer: Yes, immediate or early skin-to-skin contact probably increases infant axillary temperature, but the difference is not clinically meaningful. The mean difference is 0.28 °C higher (95% CI: 0.14 higher to 0.41 higher).
